summary.intent: Investigate and fix database lock contention on the host, layer_stat, subscription, and transactionid tables in the OpenCue scheduler (Rust) and cuebot (Java) systems.
summary.outcome: Identified subscription hot-row updates as the root cause (Theory 1) and implemented a deferred in-memory accounting solution in the Rust scheduler that batches subscription DB writes into a background flush cycle instead of updating on every frame dispatch.

repo: The Rust scheduler's AllocationService already used an in-memory cache (booked_cores) for bookability checks — the DB subscription write was redundant for scheduling decisions.
The proc table is the ground truth for active bookings; subscription.int_cores is a denormalized counter that can be reconstructed from proc via recompute_booked_from_proc().

A BEFORE ROW trigger (verify_subscription) fires on every subscription UPDATE when int_burst is unchanged and int_cores increases — using LEAST(int_cores + delta, int_burst) in the flush query prevents the trigger from ever raising an exception.
Java's procCreated() (ProcDaoJdbc.java:601-665) holds row-level locks on host and subscription across 7 sequential table updates in one transaction, compounding contention.
Java uses SELECT FOR UPDATE NOWAIT for host locking while Rust uses pg_try_advisory_lock — these don't conflict at the lock level but still race at the UPDATE step on the same host row.

code.finding: UPDATE_SUBSCRIPTION SQL and its execution were removed from update_resources() — subscription is no longer written per frame dispatch.
Added recompute_booked_from_proc() (JOINs proc→host→alloc→subscription) and flush_subscription_delta() (uses LEAST to cap at burst, preventing trigger exceptions).

Flush failures now panic (after user feedback) rather than logging an error, because a failed flush followed by a cache refresh from stale DB values would silently enable overbooking. Panic forces restart and self-healing via proc-based cache reconstruction.
show_id, alloc_id, alloc_name, core_delta, gpu_delta must be captured from virtual_proc before it is moved into dispatch_virtual_proc; record_booking() is called after successful commit.

verify_subscription trigger fires BEFORE UPDATE on subscription FOR EACH ROW when int_burst unchanged and int_cores increases — runs PL/pgSQL while the row lock is held, and on exception the lock is held through the full rollback path.

workflow: When a DB flush failure can corrupt in-memory state that persists across the error (causing silent overbooking), panic + restart is preferable to error logging — especially when startup has a self-healing reconstruction path.

For hot-row contention, check whether the DB write is actually required for correctness or is just a denormalized counter that can be batched — in-memory caches often already provide the necessary consistency for the write path.
The allocation refresh interval (CONFIG.queue.allocation_refresh_interval, ~3s) sets the staleness bound for the deferred flush approach.

friction: Multiple read iterations of actor.rs were needed before having enough context to implement the change safely (virtual_proc ownership/move issue).
The verify_subscription trigger was not initially considered in the solution design and required a follow-up redesign of the flush query to use LEAST() to avoid trigger exceptions.
open_items: Concurrent flush from multiple scheduler instances is handled by PostgreSQL row-level serialization, but this has not been load-tested — worth verifying under high-concurrency scenarios.
The ~3s staleness window for CueGUI and other external readers of subscription.int_cores is acceptable but should be documented for operators.
layer_stat read-write contention (Theory 4) and the overbooking/retry issue (Theory 5) were identified but not addressed — may still contribute to lock metrics at very high throughput.
Java cuebot's long multi-table transaction in procCreated() (Theory 2) was not addressed — still holds host and subscription locks across 7 steps.

The recompute_booked_from_proc() failure on startup currently falls back to subscription table values with a warning rather than panicking — consistency with the flush panic policy should be revisited.
